teamObjective.explanation: I am leading the first trick of a new round. My hand contains only low non-trump clubs (3-6), so the objective is to lead cheaply, retain slightly higher cards for future tricks, and give partner the best chance to take control without committing my strongest card.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 cards and has made no public plays. Because the deal is random and no cards have been revealed, I cannot assert partner's holdings. It is plausible but not guaranteed that partner has at least one card that can beat a club 3, since partner has three chances among the unseen deck.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards, which publicly signals they are near finishing, while opponent player 3 has 3 cards. No cards have been played, so their strengths are unknown. It is probable that at least one opponent can beat a club 3 with a higher non-trump or a trump, especially because a player near finish may have retained strong cards, but this remains probabilistic rather than a fact.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the minimum club 3 minimizes my investment in an opening trick where opponents likely have higher cards or trumps. It preserves the 4, 5, and 6 for later tricks when fewer high cards remain and when rank may matter more. Given my hand is all low non-trumps, there is little upside to leading higher.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is my highest non-trump card, so playing it now risks wasting my best card on a trick opponents can likely take with a higher card or trump. Saving it gives me more flexibility in later tricks.
The 5 is stronger than the 3 but still a low non-trump. Playing it now would commit a moderately useful card without increasing the chance of winning the trick meaningfully.
The 4 is slightly stronger than the 3, but the 3 is cheaper to lead and keeps the 4 as an extra step of strength for future plays.

risks: An opponent may play a higher non-trump single and win the trick.
An opponent may use a trump card to take the trick since I hold no trumps.
The near-finish opponent (player 1) may have retained strong cards and could win the trick immediately.
Partner may be unable to beat the club 3, causing our team to lose the lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the minimum card, I limit the value lost if the trick is lost and maximize the chance that partner can take over with a modest card. I retain the 4, 5, and 6 for later rounds where they may win tricks or support partner.
